meta.name: Rural Hospital Triage Rules
sources: WHO Emergency Triage Assessment and Treatment (ETAT)
meta.principle: When multiple rules match, use the HIGHEST priority. Age < 5 and age > 60 escalate any non-red case by one level.
meta.note: This is decision-support, not a substitute for clinical judgment.
limitations: No vital signs (BP, SpO2, pulse, temperature) — triage is symptom-based only.
Uncovered red flags: hemoptysis (coughing blood), bloody diarrhea, anaphylaxis, choking, chemical eye injury.
Injury severity is keyword-dependent — 'severe injury' without specific keywords defaults to yellow.
Burns of all sizes are red — small superficial burns are over-triaged.
Psychiatric complaints and pain severity are not modeled.

rules.description: Chest pain — possible cardiac emergency
Respiratory distress — possible airway compromise
Altered consciousness — neurological emergency
Severe hemorrhage — shock risk
Active seizure — airway and brain injury risk
Toxic exposure — time-critical antidote
Severe dehydration — shock and organ failure risk
Severe headache with neck stiffness — possible meningitis
Burn injury — airway and fluid loss risk
Abdominal pain in child under 5 — possible intussusception or appendicitis
Fever — possible sepsis, malaria, or typhoid
Vomiting — dehydration and electrolyte risk
Diarrhea — dehydration and infectious cause
Persistent cough — possible pneumonia or TB
Rash — possible measles, dengue, or allergic reaction
Major trauma — fracture or crush injury
Fall in elderly patient — high risk for hip fracture or intracranial bleed
Minor trauma — wound, sprain, or soft tissue injury
Headache — possible migraine, tension, or early infection
Ear pain — possible otitis media or externa
Eye complaint — possible infection, foreign body, or glaucoma
Obstetric emergency — maternal and fetal risk
Routine follow-up — stable chronic condition
Minor superficial injury — no systemic concern
Medication refill — no acute complaint
Administrative request — no clinical need
